justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7, with BR-3/BR-4 covering the cancellation role split). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Note lock (BR-7) marked invariant: false with nuanced justification distinguishing the concept from the threshold — acceptable. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the 4 minimum. Role-based cancellation correctly split into patient (BR-3) and admin (BR-4) rules. Confidence at 72 with clearly stated assumptions about license meaning, time boundaries, and treating physician scope.

observations.text: Exceptional ubiquitous language section with 12 well-defined terms covering all domain concepts
BR-7 (note lock) marked invariant: false with careful reasoning — the immutability concept is acknowledged as important while the 48h threshold is correctly identified as policy
12 acceptance criteria covering all required scenarios plus concurrency, boundary conditions, and patient self-access
8 edge cases identified including race conditions, DST transitions, back-to-back booking boundary, and license revocation — well beyond expectations
Cancellation rule correctly decomposed into two separate rules (BR-3 patient, BR-4 admin) with different invariant classifications
Confidence level 72 with specific high/low confidence areas and 6 explicit assumptions — well-calibrated
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations for genuine spec gaps

BR-7 note lock could arguably be invariant: true for clinical record integrity — marking it false is defensible but the rubric expected invariant: true for record lock. However the justification is strong enough that this is a reasonable interpretation rather than a misclassification

trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (plus a 7th reinforcing license verification). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-7, record visibility BR-5, note locking BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Cancellation split into patient (BR-3, invariant: false) and admin (BR-4, invariant: false) with clear role boundary. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation semantics, treating physician scope, and boundary conditions.

observations.text: Correctly distinguished regulatory invariants (license, privacy, note locking) from operational policies (cancellation time window, admin override scope)
BR-3 and BR-4 cleanly separate the patient vs admin cancellation role boundary with correct invariant: false classification and clear justification
12 acceptance criteria with precise concrete values (e.g., 10:00-10:30 slots, 47h vs 49h boundary tests, adjacent slot test)
Excellent edge cases including concurrency on booking, DST impact on note locking, in-flight edits at lock boundary, and license revocation with existing appointments
Well-structured escalations identifying genuine ambiguities (2h boundary inclusivity, treating physician temporal scope, note lock absoluteness) with actionable recommendations
Confidence level appropriately calibrated at 72 with explicit low-confidence areas and assumptions

trials.justification: All 6 expected rules identified (split across 8 well-structured rules), with correct invariant classifications for regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-2, record visibility BR-6, note lock immutability BR-8 all marked invariant: true). The cancellation rules correctly capture the role boundary (patient vs admin in BR-4/BR-5) and classify the time threshold as invariant: false. 13 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios. Confidence level of 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation semantics, treating physician scope, and timestamp boundaries.

observations.text: Exceptional rule decomposition — split the 48h note lock into two rules (BR-7 for the time threshold, BR-8 for immutability), correctly marking the threshold as policy (invariant: false) and the immutability as regulatory (invariant: true)
13 acceptance criteria with precise boundary conditions (e.g., AC-4 adjacent slots, AC-6 exact 2-hour boundary, AC-13 concurrent booking race)
Rich edge cases section identifying real ambiguities: license revocation, mid-edit locking, timezone handling, per-appointment vs per-patient record scope
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the treating physician scope ambiguity
Ubiquitous language section is thorough and domain-appropriate

BR-7 (48h window) marked invariant: false is debatable — the justification acknowledges the concept is invariant-class but classifies based on the threshold being configurable. Acceptable reasoning but could be argued either way
Bounded context identified as a single combined context ('Appointment Scheduling & Clinical Records') rather than two separate bounded contexts as expected, though both domains are thoroughly covered
